summary:
The Ilkhanate (1256–1335 AD) was the southwestern khanate of the fragmented [[event:91|Mongol Empire]], established by Hulagu Khan (grandson of [[event:2225|Genghis Khan]]) following his campaigns that [[event:2228|sacked Baghdad]] (1258) and attempted to conquer Egypt (defeated at [[event:2978|Ain Jalut]], 1260). Controlling Persia, Iraq, Anatolia, and parts of the Caucasus, the Ilkhanate was the most culturally complex of the Mongol successor states — ruling over ancient Persian, Arab, and Armenian civilizations with a Mongol military elite that gradually adapted to its subjects' cultures.

The Ilkhanate's history was defined by the tension between Buddhist-shamanist Mongol traditions and the predominantly Muslim Persian and Arab population. The early Ilkhans were tolerant of all religions — Hulagu's wife was a Nestorian Christian, and the Ilkhans maintained relations with European Crusader states as potential anti-Mamluk allies — but the conversion of Ghazan Khan to Islam in 1295 aligned the dynasty with its majority subjects and initiated a Persian cultural synthesis. Under Ghazan and his successors, Persian language and artistic traditions dominated the Ilkhanate court; the 'Compendium of Chronicles' (Jami' al-tawarikh) by Rashid al-Din — a world history commissioned by Ghazan — became one of the most ambitious historical and cultural projects of the medieval Islamic world. The Ilkhanate's Persian-Mongol artistic synthesis (particularly in manuscript illumination and architecture) established styles that influenced subsequent Persian, Ottoman, and Mughal art. The dynasty dissolved after 1335 due to lack of heirs and political fragmentation.
